perturbation.original_text: 5. (a) For PCII - (1) Upon the completion of my engagement as an employee, consultant, or subcontractor under the contract, or the completion of my work on the PCII Program, whichever occurs first, I will surrender promptlyto the PCII Program Manager or his designee, or to the appropriate PCII officer, PCII of any type whatsoeverthat is in my possession.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. (a) For PCII - (1) Upon the completion of my engagement as an employee, consultant, or subcontractor under the contract, or the completion of my work on the PCII Program, whichever occurs first, I will surrender PCII of any type whatsoever that is in my possession within 90 days.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the surrender requirement to 'within 90 days' after the completion of engagement introduces a contradiction. The original text implies immediate surrender, while the modified text allows a delay, which may conflict with regulations requiring prompt safeguarding of sensitive information.
perturbation.law_explanation: This law requires agencies to establish rules of conduct for personnel involved in maintaining systems of records and to provide sanctions for failing to comply with the rules. Allowing a 90-day delay in surrendering PCII could violate this section if it is not compliant with an agency's established rules for safeguarding sensitive information.

Records maintained on individuals (a) Definitions .-For purposes of this section- (1) the term "agency" means agency as defined in section 552(e) 1 of this title; (2) the term "individual" means a citizen of the United States or an alien lawfully admitted for permanent residence; (3) the term "maintain" includes maintain, collect, use, or disseminate; (4) the term "record" means any item, collection, or grouping of information about an individual that is maintained by an agency, including, but not limited to, his education, financial transactions, medical history, and criminal or employment history and that contains his name, or the identifying number, symbol, or other identifying particular assigned to the individual, such as a finger or voice print or a photograph; (5) the term "system of records" means a group of any records under the control of any agency from which information is retrieved by the name of the individual or by some identifying number, symbol, or other identifying particular assigned to the individual; (6) the term "statistical record" means a record in a system of records maintained for statistical research or reporting purposes only and not used in whole or in part in making any determination about an identifiable individual, except as provided by section 8 of title 13 ; (7) the term "routine use" means, with respect to the disclosure of a record, the use of such record for a purpose which is compatible with the purpose for which it was collected; (8) the term "matching program"- (A) means any computerized comparison of- (i) two or more automated systems of records or a system of records with non-Federal records for the purpose of- (I) establishing or verifying the eligibility of, or continuing compliance with statutory and regulatory requirements by, applicants for, recipients or beneficiaries of, participants in, or providers of services with respect to, cash or in-kind assistance or payments under Federal benefit programs, or (II) recouping payments or delinquent debts under such Federal benefit programs, or (ii) two or more automated Federal personnel or payroll systems of records

perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces a 90-day delay in surrendering PCII, contradicting the original text's implied immediate surrender. The Law Explanation suggests this delay could violate agency rules for safeguarding sensitive information. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 (5 USC 552a) broadly defines agency responsibilities regarding records, it doesn't explicitly address surrender timelines. Scraped Law Snippet 2 is a dead link. However, the Perturbation Explanation highlights the conflict between immediate surrender and a 90-day delay, suggesting a violation of regulations requiring prompt safeguarding of sensitive information. Since the promptness is undermined, a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: 7. I hereby agree that I shall promptly report to the appropriate official, in accordance with the guidance issued for the applicable category of information, any loss, theft, misuse, misplacement, unauthorized disclosure, or other securityviolation, I have knowledge of and whether or not I am personally involved.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. I hereby agree that I shall report to the appropriate official any loss, theft, misuse, misplacement, unauthorized disclosure, or other security violation, I have knowledge of and whether or not I am personally involved within 30 days of discovery.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the reporting of security violations to within 30 days contradicts the requirement for prompt reporting, potentially hindering timely mitigation and violating security regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: This law mandates agencies to have policies and procedures for responding to security incidents. Delaying the reporting of security incidents for 30 days could violate these policies requiring prompt action to mitigate risks and damages.

perturbation.justification: The original text requires immediate reporting of security violations, while the changed text allows for a 30-day delay. This contradicts the federal agency responsibilities outlined in 44 USC 3554, which mandates information security protections commensurate with the risk and magnitude of harm resulting from unauthorized access, use, disclosure, disruption, modification, or destruction of information. The 30-day delay undermines the prompt action needed to mitigate risks and damages, potentially resulting in non-compliance with federal law. The changed text weakens the original requirement for prompt reporting, creating an inconsistency that would likely fail legal scrutiny.

perturbation.original_text: I understand that if I violate the terms and conditions of this Agreement, I could be subjected to administrative, disciplinary, civil, or criminal action, as appropriate, under the laws, regulations, or directives applicable to the category of information involved and neither the United States Government nor the Authorized Entity have waived anystatutory or common law evidentiary privileges or protections that they may assert in any administrative or court proceedingto protect any sensitive information to which I have been given conditional access under the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: I understand that if I violate the terms and conditions of this Agreement, I could be subjected to disciplinary action only, under the laws, regulations, or directives applicable to the category of information involved and the United States Government and the Authorized Entity have waived any statutory or common law evidentiary privileges or protections that they may assert in any administrative or court proceedingto protect any sensitive information to which I have been given conditional access under the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the consequences of violation to 'disciplinary action only' and waiving evidentiary privileges contradicts the potential for civil or criminal action, which are often applicable in cases involving the mishandling of sensitive government information. Waiving evidentiary privileges can hinder the prosecution of violations.
perturbation.law_explanation: This law outlines criminal penalties for the unauthorized removal and retention of classified documents or materials. Limiting consequences to disciplinary action and waiving evidentiary privileges directly contradicts the possibility of criminal prosecution under this statute.

Unauthorized removal and retention of classified documents or material (a) Whoever, being an officer, employee, contractor, or consultant of the United States, and, by virtue of his office, employment, position, or contract, becomes possessed of documents or materials containing classified information of the United States, knowingly removes such documents
perturbation.justification: The changed text limits the consequences of violating the agreement to "disciplinary action only" and waives evidentiary privileges. This contradicts the possibility of criminal prosecution under 18 U.S.C. § 1924, which addresses the unauthorized removal and retention of classified documents or materials. The original text allows for 'administrative, disciplinary, civil, or criminal action,' while the change explicitly limits this to disciplinary action. By waiving evidentiary privileges, the changed text further hinders the ability to prosecute any violations, directly conflicting with the statute's intent to penalize unauthorized handling of classified information as evidenced in Scraped Law Snippet 1. The original contract preserves the possibility of criminal penalties for unauthorized removal and retention of classified documents, while the changed contract removes that option.

perturbation.original_text: 11. Unless and until I am released in writing by an authorized representative of the Department of Homeland Security (if permissible for the particular category of information), I understand that all conditions and obligations imposed upon me bythis Agreement apply during the time that I am granted conditional access, and at all times thereafter.
perturbation.changed_text: 11. All conditions and obligations imposed upon me by this Agreement apply during the time that I am granted conditional access, and for only one year thereafter, regardless of whether I am released in writing by an authorized representative of the Department of Homeland Security.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the duration of the agreement to one year contradicts the indefinite obligation to protect sensitive information even after access is terminated, as implied by the original text. Certain information may require protection beyond one year.
perturbation.law_explanation: This statute addresses the safeguarding of classified information and emphasizes the continued responsibility of individuals with access to such information. Limiting the agreement to one year after access termination could contradict the requirement for lifelong protection of certain types of classified information.

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a one-year limitation on the agreement's duration, regardless of a release from the Department of Homeland Security. This directly contradicts the implied original text's indefinite obligation to protect sensitive information. The LLM's Law Explanation states that limiting the agreement to one year "could contradict the requirement for lifelong protection of certain types of classified information." Although Scraped Law Snippet 2 is a 'Page Not Found,' Scraped Law Snippet 1, 50 USC 3162, discusses 'access to classified information,' and the need to safeguard this information, reinforcing the potential for lifelong protection. The one-year limit in the modified text creates a clear inconsistency, making the contractual terms potentially unenforceable due to the possible requirement of lifelong protection.

perturbation.original_text: 17. I have read this Agreement carefully and my questions, if any, have been answered. I acknowledge that the briefing officer has made available to me any laws, regulations, or directives referenced in this document so that Imay read them at this time, if I so choose.
perturbation.changed_text: 17. I have read this Agreement carefully. My questions, if any, will be answered within 30 days. I acknowledge that the briefing officer will provide any laws, regulations, or directives referenced in this document within 60 days.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the answering of questions and access to referenced laws/regulations introduces a contradiction. The original text implies immediate access to this information for informed consent before signing, whereas the modification allows significant delays.
perturbation.law_explanation: 5 USC 552 is the Freedom of Information Act (FOIA). While not directly applicable to pre-contractual information, it establishes the principle of timely access to government information. Delaying access to referenced laws and regulations for 60 days before the agreement takes effect undermines the principle of informed consent and immediate access to information relevant to the agreement. Awaiting 30 days to get questions answered is likewise problematic.

perturbation.justification: The modification introduces delays in answering questions and providing access to relevant laws/regulations. The original text implies immediate access for informed consent, whereas the modified text allows delays of 30 and 60 days, respectively. This directly undermines the principle of informed consent at the time of signing the agreement. Although 5 USC 552, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, is about public information and not directly about pre-contractual information, the Law Explanation correctly points out that it establishes the principle of timely access to government information which is relevant to the context of this agreement and supports the idea that access to information should not be unreasonably delayed. Scraped Law Snippet 2 is a 'Page Not Found' error; therefore, I cannot use it in my justification.
